boundary.summary: Scope correctly isolates Electron path/identity APIs behind PlatformPaths interface. The ~54 call count in the plan is inflated (~33 actual calls across 9 files confirmed via grep), but this doesn't affect the design materially. Correctly identifies store.ts, constants.ts, app-config.ts as the priority consumers and defers logging.ts, server.ts, migrate.ts, apps.ts to a future wave.
evidence: packages/desktop/src/main/logging.ts — 11 calls to app.getPath/getVersion/getName/isPackaged
packages/desktop/src/main/index.ts — 10 calls to app.getPath/isPackaged/getVersion
packages/desktop/src/main/migrate.ts — 4 calls to app.getPath/isPackaged
packages/desktop/src/main/store.ts — 2 calls (app.getPath, rationale comment about app.setPath timing)
packages/desktop/src/main/constants.ts — 1 call (app.isPackaged at module load time)
packages/desktop/src/main/app-config.ts — 1 call (app.isPackaged)
findings: Interface does not cover app.setName() or app.getAppPath(), used in index.ts lines 133-134. setPath is covered; setName is not. If the goal is full Electron-agnosticism for index.ts, this is a gap. If the goal is narrowly config resolution only, this is correct boundary discipline.

index.ts uses app.commandLine.appendSwitch (packaged-dependent, line 160), app.requestSingleInstanceLock, app.setAsDefaultProtocolClient — these are Electron lifecycle concerns correctly excluded from the interface.

coupling.summary: PlatformPaths creates a conceptually clean seam. However, two structural coupling concerns emerge from the existing code that the plan does not address.
evidence: packages/desktop/src/main/constants.ts:12 — `export const UPDATER_ENABLED = app.isPackaged && CHANNEL !== "dev"` is a module-level const evaluated at import time
packages/desktop/src/main/index.ts:237 — UPDATER_ENABLED is imported as a value and used directly in IPC handler registration
packages/desktop/src/main/updater.ts:12 — `if (!UPDATER_ENABLED) return` guards use the module-level const
packages/desktop/src/main/menu.ts:42 — UPDATER_ENABLED consumed as module-level value
findings: CRITICAL: constants.ts exports UPDATER_ENABLED as a module-level const. It cannot accept PlatformPaths as a parameter. Converting it to a deferred evaluation (function call or lazy getter) changes its type signature, requiring all 4 importing files (index.ts, updater.ts, menu.ts, and constants.ts itself) to be refactored. The plan says 'Accept PlatformPaths instead of direct app.isPackaged' without explaining how to resolve this structural conflict.

store.ts:6 — `const cache = new Map<string, Store>()` is a module-level cache. If getStore() were to receive PlatformPaths at call time, the cache retains the correct late-bound behavior. But if the cache key is name-only (which it is), two PlatformPaths instances pointing to different userData dirs would collide. Acceptable for current use case but worth documenting.

3 consumers (store.ts: app import, constants.ts: app import, app-config.ts: app import) all import from 'electron'. After refactor, store.ts and app-config.ts would drop that import; constants.ts cannot without the module-level const architectural change.

safety.summary: Low risk for existing Electron path — ElectronPlatformPaths is a thin wrapper delegating to the same app.* calls. BrowserPlatformPaths is new code, dead until explicitly wired. One subtle timing risk identified.
evidence: packages/desktop/src/main/store.ts:8-11 — Comment documents that module import hoisting prevents Store instantiation before app.setPath('userData') has run
packages/desktop/src/main/index.ts:135-139 — app.setPath('userData', ...) runs inside the Effect.gen, after import time
packages/desktop/src/main/constants.ts:12 — UPDATER_ENABLED evaluated at module load time, before app.setPath and app.setName have run
findings: UPDATER_ENABLED is evaluated at module load time (constants.ts:12) when constants.ts is first imported. If index.ts is refactored to set PlatformPaths-based isPackaged before importing constants.ts, the import order dependency changes. Currently, constants.ts is imported at the top of index.ts (line 16), guaranteeing it runs before the Effect.gen body. Any refactoring that moves ElectronPlatformPaths instantiation after the imports block would break the current timing model.
No existing tests for store.ts, constants.ts, or app-config.ts (search found no test files for these). Regression detection is manual.
ElectronPlatformPaths wraps app.getPath() which is synchronous and reliable in Electron main process — no behavioral change risk.

reversibility.summary: Changes are additive. Deleting platform-config.ts and reverting constructor parameter additions is straightforward. Reversal does not cascade beyond the 4 modified files.
evidence: Plan specifies 'additive only' modifications to existing files
Only 1 new file (platform-config.ts) created; 4 existing files modified
All modified files are in same package (packages/desktop/src/main/)
No cross-package import chain from the new interface
findings: If a future wave (Phase 2: logging.ts, server.ts, migrate.ts, apps.ts) removes the old `import { app } from 'electron'` after wiring PlatformPaths, reversibility degrades. The plan should document that Phase 2 must preserve old imports until the platform-config.ts revert is also performed.

Currently each consumer imports from 'electron' independently. The additive refactor adds an optional PlatformPaths parameter. The old path remains functional. This is architecturally clean for rollback.

surface_area.summary: Minimal and well-contained surface area. Only two new exports (PlatformPaths interface, PlatformPathKey type) — both internal to the desktop main package.
evidence: New exports: PlatformPaths (interface), PlatformPathKey (type alias)
No new exports from existing files (modifications are parameter additions only)
No change to the IPC boundary (preload.ts, ipc-channels.ts are untouched)
No change to the renderer process
No change to cross-package API boundary
findings: No issues identified.

convention.summary: The plan introduces a new architectural pattern (interface + implementation + DI) into a codebase that currently has zero interfaces and zero implementation patterns in the main directory.
evidence: smart_grep for `interface \w+ {` in packages/desktop/src/main/*.ts returns 0 matches (only env.d.ts has interfaces)
smart_grep for `implements \w+` in packages/desktop/src returns 0 matches across entire desktop package
smart_grep for `Context.Tag` and `Effect.(service|provide|layer)` in packages/desktop/src/main returns 0 matches
Existing code pattern: module-level exports with direct Electron imports (e.g., `export function getStore()`, `export const UPDATER_ENABLED`)
findings: This is the FIRST interface pattern and FIRST dependency injection pattern in the desktop main codebase. The plan does not acknowledge this deviation or justify it.

The codebase uses Effect.ts extensively in index.ts (Effect.gen, Effect.forkChild, Deferred, Fiber). An Effect Service (`Context.Tag<PlatformPaths>()` + `Layer`) would be more idiomatic than a bare TypeScript interface passed as constructor parameters. The plan should either adopt Effect Services or explain why a plain interface is preferred.

The plan's chosen pattern (constructor parameter injection) conflicts with the existing module-level export pattern. Example: `export function getStore()` currently takes no arguments. Adding a PlatformPaths parameter changes every call site. This is acknowledged in the plan but the migration cost for the 4 call sites in index.ts is not enumerated.
UPDATER_ENABLED being a module-level const (constants.ts:12) consumed by 4 files as a value means converting to deferred evaluation is a deeper refactor than the plan suggests.

resilience.summary: BrowserPlatformPaths does not function in the deployment context where platform-config.ts lives (Electron main process / Node.js). No graceful degradation strategy is defined.
evidence: platform-config.ts is in packages/desktop/src/main/ — this runs in Electron's main process (Node.js, not a browser context)
BrowserPlatformPaths uses 'localStorage, navigator, document.baseURI' — none of these are available in Node.js
PlatformPaths.getPath(key) returns `string` — no error/undefined path for 'unavailable' scenarios
No fallback strategy for BrowserPlatformPaths when browser APIs are unavailable
The plan asserts 'won't affect existing Electron path' (true) but doesn't address what happens when BrowserPlatformPaths IS used
findings: BrowserPlatformPaths cannot be instantiated in the main process. If the deployment target is the renderer process, the plan must explain how platform-config.ts (currently in src/main/) would be imported into renderer context — this requires either the preload bridge or a separate browser-only bundle.

The BrowserPlatformPaths implementation uses local storage and navigator APIs that have no Node.js equivalents. No fallback mechanism (e.g., os.homedir(), os.tmpdir()) is documented for cases where browser APIs are absent.
The interface returns `string` for all paths with no option for 'unavailable'. A more resilient design would return `string | null` or throw a structured error type.
No test plan for verifying BrowserPlatformPaths behavior in isolation.
If BrowserPlatformPaths is intended for testing only, the plan should say so explicitly rather than presenting it as a production fallback.

conditions_for_approval: 1. [constants.ts coupling] — The plan must specify how UPDATER_ENABLED's module-level evaluation is resolved. Options: (a) convert to a function `isUpdaterEnabled(paths: PlatformPaths): boolean` and update all 4 consumers, or (b) defer this to a separate follow-up slice and document the limitation. The plan cannot claim 'Accept PlatformPaths in constants.ts' without addressing this structural barrier.

2. [Effect convention] — Either adopt Effect Context.Tag/Layer pattern (more idiomatic for this codebase) OR add a rationale section explaining why a plain TypeScript interface + constructor injection is preferred over Effect Services. The review finds the plain interface acceptable but the decision must be explicit.

3. [BrowserPlatformPaths deployment] — The plan must specify the target deployment context for BrowserPlatformPaths (main process, renderer, or testing only). If it's meant for the main process, implement fallback Node.js APIs (os.homedir(), os.tmpdir()) instead of browser-only APIs. Add graceful `getPath()` fallback behavior.

4. [Testability] — Add at least one test for ElectronPlatformPaths to validate it delegates correctly. The grep search found `shell-env.test.ts` (the only test in src/main/) and 2 tests in tests/ — no existing coverage for store, constants, or app-config. This refactor is an opportunity to add coverage.
recommendations: Add `getAppPath(): string` to the PlatformPaths interface to cover index.ts's `app.getPath('appData')` usage (line 137).
Document that the module-level cache in store.ts (line 6) couples to a single userData path — if multiple PlatformPaths instances are ever needed, the cache key must include the path.
Add `Effect.withSpan` annotations on the new interface methods for debuggability, which is consistent with the Effect usage pattern in index.ts.
Explicitly mark BrowserPlatformPaths as `@internal` or document its limited use scope to prevent misuse.
Enumerate the 4 call sites of getStore() in index.ts that would need the new PlatformPaths parameter (lines 241, 243, 244, 249 via server.ts).

follow_up_issues.title: Electron app.getVersion/isPackaged calls in updater.ts and windows.ts
follow_up_issues.description: updater.ts has 2 app.getVersion() calls (lines 24, 42) and windows.ts has 1 call (app.getPath('userData') at an unconfirmed line). These are not in the current scope but should be migrated in Phase 2.

follow_up_issues.title: PlatformPaths-aware test infrastructure
follow_up_issues.description: No tests exist for store.ts, constants.ts, or app-config.ts (the 3 files the plan touches most). Create an ElectronPlatformPaths spy/fake for testing and add coverage as part of this refactor.

follow_up_issues.title: Re-evaluate app.setName() need in PlatformPaths
follow_up_issues.description: index.ts:133 calls app.setName(app.isPackaged ? APP_NAMES[CHANNEL] : 'OpenCode Dev'). This uses isPackaged which is now in PlatformPaths, but setName is an Electron API. If index.ts needs full Electron independence, setName must either be added to PlatformPaths or the name-setting deferred until after ElectronPlatformPaths is injected.

summary: The plan correctly identifies the seam and proposes a well-scoped refactoring slice. It is APPROVED WITH CONDITIONS. The 3 blocking issues are: (1) resolving constants.ts's module-level `UPDATER_ENABLED` const which cannot accept PlatformPaths injection, (2) documenting why a plain TypeScript interface is chosen over an Effect Service given the codebase's heavy Effect usage, and (3) specifying the deployment target and fallback strategy for BrowserPlatformPaths (currently uses browser-only APIs but lives in the Node.js main process directory). The convention score of 2/5 reflects that this is a new architectural pattern for the codebase — this is not a design flaw but needs explicit justification. Resilience requires the most attention before BrowserPlatformPaths can be considered production-ready.
